source_title: Towards bridging the gap: Systematic sim-to-real transfer for diverse legged robots

ai_summary.one_liner: PACE fits a 4n+1 physically interpretable joint-dynamics model from about 20 seconds of encoder-only in-air data, enabling dynamics-randomization-free zero-shot locomotion across diverse robots while reducing ANYmal's full Cost of Transport by 32%.
ai_summary.problem: Sim-trained legged locomotion policies often fail or waste energy on hardware because actuator and system dynamics are mismatched; common fixes require broad dynamics randomization, torque sensors, black-box actuator networks, or complex hand-tuned rewards.
ai_summary.solution: Collect fixed-base chirp trajectories, fit per-joint armature, viscous damping, Coulomb friction, joint bias, and one global delay with massively parallel CMA-ES, then train an asymmetric PPO policy with four reward terms including a physics-grounded PMSM energy loss.
ai_summary.key_result: Using about 20 seconds of encoder-only data, PACE transfers without dynamics randomization across three primary and more than ten additional robots; ANYmal D reaches CoT 1.27 versus 1.86 for the prior ANYmal C baseline (32% reduction), while Tytan reaches CoT 0.97 and 6.10 km per charge.
limitations: The fixed-base identification has finite excitation bandwidth and assumes sufficiently rigid structures, correct kinematics, consistent firmware compensation, and modest nonlinearities.
Effective parameters drift with temperature, wear, aging, filters, firmware, and simulator changes.
Contact compliance, electrical bus/current limits, inverter switching, and higher-order dynamics are not yet explicitly identified.
Parameter optimization still takes roughly 10–24 hours per robot on a single GPU despite requiring little real data.

research_gaps.question: Can compact joint-space identification remain sufficient for highly compliant, contact-rich, or deformable-terrain locomotion?
How should electrical constraints and power-electronics losses be co-identified with mechanical dynamics so that high-speed policies exploit the true hardware envelope safely?
Can lightweight online adaptation track temperature, wear, aging, and firmware drift without sacrificing the interpretability and stability of the fitted model?

future_directions: Combine CMA-ES with local gradient refinement in differentiable physics.
Add contact-parameter refinement using force sensing or force plates.
Fit bus-voltage, current-limit, and inverter-switching behavior jointly with mechanical parameters.
Model joint and foot compliance, link flexibility, and series elasticity explicitly.
Use lightweight online adaptation to track wear and temperature drift.
Extend the model with higher-order motion terms to capture bandwidth limits and smooth high-frequency locomotion.

relations.note: Both target fast, reliable zero-shot sim-to-real locomotion with compact rewards; PACE replaces dynamics randomization with identified actuator/system dynamics, whereas the humanoid recipe relies on strong randomization and fast off-policy RL.

Both study learned legged locomotion transferred from simulation to hardware, but PACE focuses on actuator/system alignment and energy while the parkour work focuses on perception and long-horizon skill composition.
PACE supplies a concrete system-identification and energy-modeling point in the broader learned legged and whole-body control landscape covered by the survey.
